policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Pre-existing Disease Exclusion
content: Pre-existing Disease (PED) means any condition, ailment, injury or disease diagnosed, or for which medical advice or treatment was received by the Insured Person within 48 months prior to the first policy issued by the insurer to the insured person. Any Medical Expense incurred for treatment of a Pre-existing Disease shall not be admissible under this policy for a period of 48 months of continuous coverage from the date of inception of the first policy.
plain_english: If you had a health condition diagnosed or treated in the 4 years before buying this insurance, it is a pre-existing disease. Claims for treating that condition will be rejected for the first 4 years of the policy. After 4 continuous years of renewing this policy, the condition is covered.
consumer_tip: Always disclose all past medical conditions truthfully in your proposal form. Non-disclosure is worse — if the insurer discovers it later, they can reject ALL claims, not just PED-related ones. If you have PED, choose an insurer with a shorter PED waiting period (some offer 2 or 3 years).

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Initial Waiting Period
content: The Company shall not be liable to make any payment under this policy in respect of any expense whatsoever incurred by any Insured Person during the first 30 days from the date of commencement of this policy, except in the case of Accidental Bodily Injury sustained by the Insured Person after the commencement of the policy.
plain_english: For the first 30 days of the policy, no medical claims are covered EXCEPT for accidental injuries. You can claim for a road accident on Day 5, but not for a fever, surgery, or any other illness. This prevents people from buying insurance only when they are already sick.
consumer_tip: If you are switching from one insurer to another (portability), the initial 30-day waiting period is waived — your previous coverage period counts. If you are buying for the first time, plan so that the 30-day window does not coincide with a known health issue.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Room Rent Sub-limit and Proportionate Deduction
content: In the event of hospitalization, the Company will cover the charges for accommodation in a hospital room up to the limit specified in the Schedule. If the Insured Person is admitted to a room with charges in excess of the eligible room rent, all other expenses (including surgeon's fees, nursing charges, ICU charges, doctor visit charges, anaesthesia charges, and operation theatre charges) shall be payable proportionately in the same ratio as the admissible room rent bears to the actual room rent charged.
plain_english: Your policy covers room rent up to a daily limit (e.g., ₹3,000/day). If you choose a room costing ₹6,000/day (double the limit), the insurer does NOT just cut the room rent difference. Instead, ALL medical charges (surgeon fees, doctor visits, nursing, OT charges) are also cut by 50% — because your room is 50% over the limit. This can result in very large out-of-pocket expenses on a big hospital bill.
consumer_tip: The proportionate deduction clause is one of the most expensive surprises in Indian health insurance. Always choose a room that matches your policy's room rent limit. Or buy a policy with NO room rent sub-limit — it costs slightly more in premium but saves you dramatically on claims. Ask specifically: 'Does this policy have a room rent sub-limit?'

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Cosmetic Surgery Exclusion
content: The Company shall not be liable to make any payment under this policy in respect of any expense incurred for treatment which is related to or arising from cosmetic or aesthetic treatment or surgery, including but not limited to: rhinoplasty (nose job), mammoplasty (breast augmentation/reduction), hair transplant, liposuction, dental implants (unless due to injury), and any procedure not medically necessary.
plain_english: Health insurance does not cover any procedures done to change or improve your appearance — even if done in a hospital by a qualified surgeon. Cosmetic surgery, hair transplants, laser skin treatments, and aesthetic procedures are excluded. If a reconstructive surgery is needed after an accident or cancer (medically necessary), that is generally covered — but confirm with your insurer.
consumer_tip: Post-cancer reconstruction (e.g., breast reconstruction after mastectomy) or reconstructive surgery after a severe accident is typically covered as it is medically necessary. If your claim is being rejected as 'cosmetic', ask the insurer to classify it — submit your treating doctor's medical necessity certificate.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Maternity Benefit
content: The Company shall be liable to pay for medical expenses incurred for normal delivery and caesarean section subject to: (a) a waiting period of [24/36/48] months from the date of commencement of the first policy year, (b) the maternity benefit limit specified in the Schedule of Benefits, (c) the insured person being a female member named in the schedule, (d) pre and post-natal charges up to the sub-limit specified.
plain_english: Maternity cover is available only after you have held the policy for a waiting period (varies: 2 to 4 years depending on the plan). You must be explicitly listed as a covered female member. The cover has a cap — typically ₹50,000 to ₹2 lakh for normal delivery or C-section. Pre and post-natal consultations are covered up to a sub-limit.
consumer_tip: If you are planning a pregnancy, buy health insurance with maternity cover IMMEDIATELY — so the waiting period is served by the time you need it. Most policies have 2–4 year waiting periods. Do not buy maternity cover when pregnant — it will not be covered. Also check if the newborn is automatically covered from birth under the same policy.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — War and Nuclear Exclusion
content: The Company shall not be liable to make any payment under this policy directly or indirectly caused by, arising from or in consequence of: (a) War, invasion, act of foreign enemies, hostilities (whether war be declared or not), civil war, rebellion, revolution, insurrection or civil commotion assuming the proportions of or amounting to an uprising; (b) Nuclear weapons or material, ionising radiation, contamination by radioactivity from nuclear material.
plain_english: Health insurance does not cover medical expenses arising from war, invasion, civil war, or nuclear events. This is a standard exclusion in virtually all insurance policies globally. If you are hospitalized due to injuries from a war situation or nuclear contamination, health insurance will not cover the treatment.
consumer_tip: This exclusion is standard and unavoidable. It is relevant only in extreme circumstances. For travel to conflict zones, standard travel health insurance is also excluded — some specialty insures offer war zone health insurance for journalists, aid workers, and military contractors.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Cataract Sub-limit
content: Notwithstanding any other provision in this policy, expenses related to treatment of cataract (ICD code H25 and H26) including surgery (phacoemulsification or extracapsular extraction with or without implant of intraocular lens) shall be admissible only up to the limit specified in the Schedule of Benefits per eye per policy year, irrespective of the actual expenses incurred.
plain_english: Even if your total sum insured is ₹10 lakh, cataract surgery is capped at a specific sub-limit — often ₹40,000 to ₹1 lakh per eye per year (depending on the plan). If the surgery at a premium eye hospital costs ₹80,000 per eye and your sub-limit is ₹40,000, you pay ₹40,000 out of pocket per eye.
consumer_tip: Before booking cataract surgery, verify the exact sub-limit in your policy schedule. Choose a hospital and lens type that fits within the sub-limit. Government or mid-tier hospitals often offer good cataract surgery within ₹40,000–₹60,000 range.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Specific Disease Waiting Period
content: Notwithstanding any other provision in this policy, the following diseases/treatments are excluded for the first [12/24] months of the policy, irrespective of whether or not such disease existed prior to the date of commencement of the policy: hernia, hydrocele, undescended testes, congenital internal diseases, cataract, benign prostatic hypertrophy, myomectomy, uterine fibroids, sinusitis and related disorders, gout and rheumatism.
plain_english: Even if you did NOT have these conditions before buying the policy, they are excluded for the first 1 or 2 years. This means if you develop a hernia in month 8, the surgery is not covered. After the waiting period ends, these conditions are covered fully — whether they are new or pre-existing.
consumer_tip: This is a 'Specific Disease Waiting Period' — different from PED waiting period. Always read the list of specific disease waiting periods in your policy schedule. These are usually non-negotiable. If you know you will need treatment for one of these conditions soon, your claim will likely be rejected during this window.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Day Care Procedures
content: The Company shall reimburse/pay Medical Expenses incurred for Day Care Treatment(s) as listed in Annexure [X] of this policy, which requires less than 24 hours of hospitalization due to advancement of medical technology. The requirement of 24-hour hospitalization does not apply to Day Care Treatment(s). Hospitalization expenses for the listed Day Care Treatments shall be payable subject to the Sum Insured and applicable sub-limits.
plain_english: Certain medical procedures that used to require 24-hour hospitalization can now be done as 'day care' — you come in the morning and go home by evening. Health insurance covers these procedures even though you were not hospitalized overnight. Examples: cataract surgery, chemotherapy sessions, dialysis, knee arthroscopy, lithotripsy.
consumer_tip: If an insurer rejects your claim for a listed day care procedure citing 'hospitalization was less than 24 hours' — this is against IRDAI guidelines. File a complaint with the insurer's grievance cell citing the IRDAI's mandatory day care procedure list. Day care claims are your right.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Mental Illness Coverage
content: In accordance with Section 21(4) of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and IRDAI Circular dated [date], the Company shall provide insurance for treatment of mental illness on the same basis as is available for physical illness. In-patient treatment for mental illness including psychiatric hospitalization, psychotherapy during hospitalization, and rehabilitation shall be covered. The coverage shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this policy including applicable waiting periods and sub-limits.
plain_english: You have a legal right to health insurance coverage for mental illness. If you are hospitalized for depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, anxiety disorder, or any other mental illness, your health insurer must cover it the same way they cover physical illnesses like heart disease or diabetes. This is mandated by law.
consumer_tip: If your insurer rejects a mental illness hospitalization claim, they are violating the Mental Healthcare Act 2017 and IRDAI circular. File a complaint immediately with IRDAI Bima Bharosa. You have a strong legal right in this case. OPD mental health (outpatient therapy) may or may not be covered — check your policy's OPD cover.

policy_name: Standard Term Life Policy — Suicide Exclusion
content: In case of death due to suicide within 12 months from the date of commencement of risk under this policy or from the date of revival of the policy, as applicable, the nominee or beneficiary of the policyholder shall be entitled to at least 80% of the total premiums paid till the date of death, or the surrender value available as on date of death, whichever is higher. The death benefit shall not be payable. This exclusion shall not apply after completion of 12 months from the date of commencement of risk.
plain_english: If the policyholder dies by suicide within the first year of the policy, the full death benefit is NOT paid. However, 80% of all premiums paid so far will be returned to the family — this is mandated by IRDAI. After the first year of the policy, suicide is NOT excluded — the full death benefit is paid.
consumer_tip: This is a commonly misunderstood clause. After the first year, suicide is covered like any other death under term plans. Insurance companies cannot use suicide as a reason to reject claims after year 1. If you are renewing a lapsed policy (revival), the 12-month window restarts from the revival date.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Non-disclosure and Misrepresentation
content: If any statement made in the proposal form or any document leading to the issue of this policy is found to be inaccurate or false, or if any material fact has been suppressed, the Company reserves the right to repudiate any claim under this policy. However, no repudiation shall be made on grounds of misrepresentation or non-disclosure after 3 years from the date of commencement of the policy or from the date of revival, as per Section 45 of the Insurance Act, 1938.
plain_english: If you lied in your proposal form (e.g., hid a pre-existing health condition), the insurer can reject your claim. BUT — after 3 years of the policy being in force, the insurer CANNOT reject a claim on the grounds of what you disclosed or did not disclose in the proposal form (except in cases of provable fraud). This is your protection under Section 45.
consumer_tip: Always disclose truthfully — the risk of non-disclosure is complete claim rejection within 3 years. After 3 years, your family is protected even if there was some non-disclosure. Never misrepresent your smoking status or medical history — insurers verify during claim investigation.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Nomination Clause
content: The Policyholder shall have the right to nominate the person(s) to whom the moneys secured by the policy shall be paid in the event of the Policyholder's death. Nomination shall be made in accordance with Section 39 of the Insurance Act, 1938. If the nominee is the policyholder's spouse, children, or parents (beneficial nominees), the nominee shall be the absolute owner of the policy moneys on the policyholder's death. In case of any other nominee, the nominee shall hold the policy moneys as a trustee for the benefit of the legal heirs.
plain_english: You can name a person (nominee) to receive the death benefit. If the nominee is your immediate family (spouse, children, parents), they are the absolute owners — your other relatives cannot claim it. If you name a non-family member (friend, distant relative), that person holds the money in trust for your legal heirs — they cannot keep it.
consumer_tip: Name your spouse or children as beneficial nominees for clean, immediate claim settlement. If you have minor children as nominees, also appoint a 'Appointee' (usually the other parent) to manage the money until the child turns 18. Update nominee details after major life events — marriage, divorce, birth of a child.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Assignment Clause
content: The Policyholder may assign or transfer the policy at any time before the policy matures, subject to Section 38 of the Insurance Act, 1938. The assignment shall be made either by an endorsement upon the policy itself or by a separate instrument signed by the transferor/assignor, as the case may be. Notice of the assignment must be submitted in writing to the Company. The Company shall acknowledge the assignment by an endorsement on the policy. In case of absolute assignment, the assignee shall have the full right and title to the policy and all benefits thereunder.
plain_english: You can transfer your life insurance policy to another person or institution (like a bank). This is often done as collateral for a home loan. There are two types: Absolute assignment (complete transfer — assignee becomes full owner) and Conditional assignment (partial transfer — ownership reverts when condition is met, e.g., loan repaid).
consumer_tip: When you take a home loan and assign your life policy to the bank, check: (1) Is it absolute or conditional? Conditional is better — if the loan is repaid, the policy returns to you. (2) Does the bank send assignment cancellation notice to the insurer after loan repayment? Failure to do this creates issues during claim settlement.

policy_name: Standard ULIP Policy — Free Look Period
content: Notwithstanding anything contained in this policy, if the Policyholder is not satisfied with the terms and conditions of the policy, the Policyholder may return the policy to the Company within 30 days from the date of receipt of the policy, stating the reasons for objection. On such cancellation, the Policyholder shall be entitled to receive the fund value as on date of cancellation plus refund of all charges deducted including mortality charges and administration charges, subject to deduction of risk premium for the period of coverage and policy stamp duty.
plain_english: You have 30 days from receiving the policy document to read it and return it if you are not happy (for ULIPs and online policies; 15 days for offline policies). You get back most of your money — the insurer only deducts the cost of coverage for the days you were insured (risk premium) and stamp duty. No surrender penalty during free look.
consumer_tip: Use the free look period seriously — read the policy document on receiving it. If anything does not match what the agent told you, return it within 30 days. Mis-selling is common in ULIPs — if you were told it is 'like an FD' or 'guaranteed returns', that is mis-selling. Free look period is your exit without penalty.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Drunk Driving Exclusion
content: The Company shall not be liable under Section I (Own Damage) or Section II (Third Party Liability) of this policy for any accident, injury, loss or damage caused, sustained or incurred whilst the vehicle is being driven by any person who at the time of the accident is under the influence of intoxicating liquor, drugs or narcotic substances, and whose blood alcohol content exceeds the permissible limit as prescribed under the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.
plain_english: If you are drunk driving (blood alcohol above the legal limit of 30mg per 100ml under India's Motor Vehicles Act) and have an accident, your motor insurance does NOT cover: (a) damage to your own vehicle, (b) third-party injuries/death. This is a hard exclusion — there are no exceptions.
consumer_tip: Not only does drunk driving void your insurance claim — it is also a criminal offense under the Motor Vehicles Act with potential imprisonment. After any accident, the police can order a breathalyzer test. A failed test means criminal charges + no insurance coverage. Never drink and drive.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Geographical Limit
content: The policy shall be operative in India only. In case the Insured desires to extend the cover to foreign countries such as Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, and Maldives, the Company may at its discretion extend the cover on payment of additional premium and on such terms and conditions as the Company may decide. The cover will be extended only for the specific country/countries and the period mentioned in the endorsement.
plain_english: Your standard motor insurance only works in India. If you drive your car to Nepal, Sri Lanka, or Bangladesh (which some Indian car owners do), your Indian motor policy does not automatically cover you there. You need a special endorsement from your insurer to extend coverage. Pakistan is practically excluded.
consumer_tip: Planning a road trip to Nepal or Bhutan? Contact your motor insurer at least 2 weeks before departure and request a 'geographical extension endorsement' for the specific country and period. There is an additional premium for this. Also carry an International Driving Permit if required.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Consequential Loss Exclusion
content: The Company shall not be liable under this policy for: (a) Consequential loss, depreciation, wear and tear, mechanical or electrical breakdown, failures or breakages; (b) Damage to tyres and tubes unless the vehicle is damaged at the same time; (c) Any accidental loss or damage suffered whilst the insured vehicle is being used other than in accordance with the 'Limitations as to Use'.
plain_english: Motor insurance does NOT cover: (1) Gradual wear and tear of your car, (2) Mechanical or electrical breakdowns, (3) Tyre damage alone (unless the whole car is damaged in the same accident), (4) Engine damage due to driving into waterlogged areas (unless you have Engine Protect add-on). Only accidental, sudden damage to the vehicle is covered.
consumer_tip: Understand what 'consequential loss' means for you: if you drove into a flooded road and your engine hydrolocked, the standard policy does NOT cover this. You need the 'Engine Protect' add-on. If your tyre bursts on the highway alone, no coverage. If an accident damages the tyre + car, both are covered.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — IDV Clause and Depreciation
content: The Insured's Declared Value (IDV) of the vehicle shall be treated as the Sum Insured for the purpose of this policy. IDV shall be fixed at the commencement of each policy year and shall be the current ex-showroom price of the vehicle (adjusted for the age of the vehicle as per the depreciation schedule). In case of total loss or constructive total loss, the Company shall pay the IDV less the applicable deductibles. In case of partial loss, depreciation on the replaced parts shall be deducted as per the depreciation schedule.
plain_english: IDV is the insured value of your car — based on the current market value adjusted for age. It decreases every year as the car ages. In a total loss (accident or theft), the insurer pays the IDV. For partial repairs, they deduct depreciation on replaced parts (e.g., plastic parts: 30% depreciation after 3 years). IDV at renewal should reflect the correct current value of your car.
consumer_tip: When renewing motor insurance, do not set IDV too low to save premium — you will be underinsured in a total loss. Also do not set it too high — you cannot profit from insurance (insurer pays only market value). A fair IDV = current market resale value of your car model and year.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Zero Depreciation Add-on Conditions
content: Subject to the terms, conditions and exclusions of this policy, and payment of additional premium, the Company agrees to indemnify the Insured against depreciation of parts in the event of an accidental loss. The depreciation clause of the standard policy shall stand waived. However, this add-on shall be subject to: (a) Maximum of [1/2/3] claims per policy year; (b) Policyholder paying the applicable compulsory deductible; (c) This add-on shall not apply if the vehicle is more than [5] years old; (d) Rubber, nylon, plastic parts, tyres and tubes shall be payable at 50% of the replaced cost under this add-on.
plain_english: Zero depreciation means no depreciation is deducted when parts are replaced — good news. But: (1) There is usually a limit on how many claims you can make with zero dep (typically 2 per year), (2) You still pay the compulsory deductible (₹1,000–₹2,000), (3) After the car is 5 years old, zero dep is usually not available. Even with zero dep, rubber, nylon, and plastic parts are covered at only 50% (not 100%).
consumer_tip: Zero depreciation is most valuable for new cars (0–5 years). After 5 years, most insurers stop offering it. Even with zero dep, plan for the compulsory deductible (₹1,000–₹2,000). Note that rubber seals, plastic trims, and tyres are still at 50% under zero dep — not fully free.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — NCB Protection Add-on
content: Subject to payment of additional premium, the No Claim Bonus (NCB) accrued under this policy shall be protected in the event of the Insured making one claim (Own Damage or Theft) during the policy year. The NCB earned in the previous year shall be applied to the renewal premium notwithstanding such a claim. This add-on is valid for a maximum of one OD claim per policy year. In the event of more than one claim, the NCB shall be forfeited in accordance with the standard NCB rules.
plain_english: Normally, making ANY claim resets your NCB (No Claim Bonus) to zero, costing you thousands more at renewal. The NCB Protection add-on lets you make ONE claim per year without losing your NCB. At renewal, you keep your 20–50% NCB discount. Making a second claim in the same year still resets the NCB.
consumer_tip: NCB Protection add-on is worth buying if you have accumulated a high NCB (35–50%) — the premium saving is large enough to justify the add-on cost. At 50% NCB, a ₹20,000 OD premium becomes ₹10,000. Losing that 50% to a small claim without protection would cost ₹10,000 more at renewal.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Adventure Sports Exclusion
content: This policy does not cover any accident, loss, liability or expense arising from the Insured Person's participation in hazardous activities or adventure sports including but not limited to: mountaineering or trekking above 4,000 metres, bungee jumping, skydiving, paragliding, hang gliding, scuba diving below 30 metres, white water rafting above Grade IV, BMX racing, motor racing, or any other activity involving substantial risk of injury or death.
plain_english: Standard travel insurance does NOT cover you if you get injured doing adventure sports — skiing, skydiving, scuba diving, paragliding, mountain climbing. If you break your leg on a ski slope or have an accident skydiving, your standard travel policy will reject the medical claim.
consumer_tip: If your trip involves adventure activities, buy a travel policy with an 'Adventure Sports Add-on' or 'Hazardous Activities extension.' These cost more but cover injuries from listed adventure activities. Always read the exclusion list — some policies cover trekking up to 4,000m but not above. Himalayan treks (above 4,000m altitude) require specialized travel insurance.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Pre-existing Condition Exclusion
content: This policy does not cover any claim directly or indirectly arising from, based upon, or attributable to any Pre-existing Condition. A Pre-existing Condition means any medical condition, disease, ailment, illness or injury for which the Insured Person has received medical treatment, consultation, diagnosis, or prescription in the 12 months prior to the inception of this policy, or any chronic condition that requires ongoing treatment.
plain_english: Travel insurance does NOT cover medical emergencies abroad that are caused by a pre-existing health condition. If you have diabetes and are hospitalized abroad for a diabetic emergency, your travel policy will reject the claim. This includes conditions under regular treatment — hypertension, asthma, diabetes, heart conditions.
consumer_tip: If you have a pre-existing condition and are traveling internationally, look for a travel policy that specifically offers 'Pre-existing Condition Cover' — some insurers offer this for stable, controlled conditions (e.g., controlled diabetes, stable hypertension). It costs more but is essential. Always carry your regular medications in sufficient quantity for the entire trip.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Baggage Loss Sub-limits
content: The Company's liability under the Baggage Loss benefit shall be subject to the limits specified in the Schedule of Benefits. Additionally, the following sub-limits shall apply irrespective of the overall baggage limit: (a) Jewellery, watches, and precious items: 10% of overall baggage limit per item, maximum [₹10,000]; (b) Electronic items and gadgets: [₹25,000] per item; (c) Business items and samples: excluded; (d) Cash, currency, and negotiable instruments: [₹5,000]. No single item shall be reimbursed for more than its purchase value.
plain_english: Even if your overall baggage loss limit is ₹1 lakh, individual items have sub-limits. Jewellery in your checked bag is covered only up to ₹10,000 per item. Electronics up to ₹25,000 per item. Cash in your bag is covered only up to ₹5,000. Business merchandise and product samples are NOT covered at all.
consumer_tip: Do not check in expensive jewellery, electronics, or large amounts of cash. Carry these in your hand baggage (which is your responsibility — generally covered only if snatched). Consider a personal belongings extension or jewellery-specific rider if you are carrying high-value items.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Trip Cancellation Covered Reasons
content: The Company shall reimburse the unused, non-refundable, pre-paid trip expenses in the event of trip cancellation due to: (a) sudden accidental bodily injury, or illness resulting in hospitalization of the Insured, Insured's spouse, or dependent children; (b) death of the Insured or Insured's immediate family member (spouse, parents, children, siblings); (c) natural disasters making the destination uninhabitable; (d) mandatory work commitment for Government employees requiring documented proof; (e) Visa denial from destination country's embassy (with proof of rejection). Trip cancellation due to personal choice, financial reasons, or disinclination to travel is NOT covered.
plain_english: Trip cancellation insurance pays only for specific, documented reasons: serious illness, death in immediate family, natural disaster at destination, or visa refusal. It does NOT pay if you simply do not want to go, if flights are late (that is flight delay, not cancellation), or if you change your mind due to fear or work pressure.
consumer_tip: Keep documentation for everything: doctor certificate with diagnosis, hospitalization records, death certificate, embassy visa refusal letter. Trip cancellation claims require proof — verbal explanations do not suffice. If your flight is cancelled by the airline, the airline bears responsibility — this is not a travel insurance cancellation claim.

policy_name: Standard PA Policy — Occupational Hazard Exclusion
content: This policy does not cover accidental death, disability or injury arising from or in connection with the Insured's engagement in: (a) Any hazardous occupation or profession not declared at inception and accepted by the Company; (b) Mining, quarrying, blasting or explosives work; (c) Electrical or underground work; (d) Military or paramilitary operations; (e) Aviation as pilot, crew or student pilot (other than as a fare-paying passenger). The Insured must declare any change in occupation to the Company within 30 days of such change.
plain_english: If your job is hazardous (mining, blasting, electrical line work, military), you need to declare this when buying PA insurance. If you do not declare it, accidents at work in a hazardous role will not be covered. Also, if you change to a more hazardous job during the policy year, you must inform the insurer within 30 days.
consumer_tip: Do not try to hide a hazardous occupation to save on premium — the premium loading for hazardous occupations is usually reasonable. If occupation changes to something riskier during the policy year, tell the insurer — they may load the premium slightly but will continue coverage. Non-disclosure of occupation leads to claim rejection.

policy_name: Standard PA Policy — Disability Definition and Schedule
content: In the event of accidental bodily injury to the Insured resulting in Permanent Total Disability, the Company shall pay 100% of the Capital Sum Insured. In the event of Permanent Partial Disability, the Company shall pay the percentage of Capital Sum Insured as specified in the Table of Disabilities: (a) Loss of two limbs or two eyes, or one limb and one eye: 100%; (b) Loss of one hand or foot: 50%; (c) Loss of one eye: 50%; (d) Loss of four fingers and thumb of one hand: 40%; (e) Loss of four fingers: 35%; (f) Loss of thumb — both phalanges: 25%; (g) Loss of index finger — three phalanges: 10%; (h) Loss of hearing in both ears: 50%; (i) Loss of hearing in one ear: 15%.
plain_english: PA insurance pays a fixed percentage of the sum assured based on which body part is permanently lost or disabled. Losing both hands or eyes = 100% payout. Losing one hand = 50% payout. Losing one eye = 50% payout. Losing a thumb = 25% payout. These percentages are standardized by IRDAI across all PA insurers.
consumer_tip: If you have a physically demanding job where hands or eyes are at risk, ensure your PA sum assured is high enough. Calculate: if you lose a hand (50% PPD payout from a ₹20 lakh policy = ₹10 lakh) — is ₹10 lakh enough to sustain your family and your rehabilitation? PA insurance is extremely cheap relative to the coverage it provides.

policy_name: Standard PA Policy — Self-inflicted Injury Exclusion
content: This policy does not cover accidental death, disability, injury or expense arising from: (a) Intentional self-inflicted injuries or suicide or attempted suicide; (b) The Insured being under the influence of alcohol, drugs, or narcotic substances; (c) The Insured engaging in any criminal or illegal act; (d) Pregnancy, childbirth, or medical termination of pregnancy (including complications thereof); (e) Mental or nervous disorder; (f) Aviation, except as a fare-paying passenger on a licensed commercial airline.
plain_english: PA insurance does NOT cover: self-harm, injuries while drunk or on drugs, injuries during criminal activities, pregnancy complications, mental illness, or non-commercial flying (you must be a paying passenger on a commercial flight to be covered for aviation accidents).
consumer_tip: PA insurance covers accidental injuries during normal daily activities — commuting, working, recreational activities (mostly). Extreme sports, hazardous activities, and above exclusions are standard across all PA policies. For sports, look for PA policies with 'sports extension' rider.

policy_name: Standard PA Policy — Temporary Total Disability (TTD) Benefit
content: In the event of accidental bodily injury to the Insured resulting in Temporary Total Disability (TTD), the Company shall pay a weekly benefit of [1% of Capital Sum Insured / specified amount per week] for the period of such disability, commencing from the eighth day after the onset of the disability and continuing for a maximum period of 104 weeks. The Insured must provide a medical certificate from a registered medical practitioner certifying the disability and its expected duration.
plain_english: If an accident leaves you completely unable to work temporarily — say you break both arms and cannot work for 3 months — PA insurance pays you a weekly income replacement during this period. Payment starts from day 8 of the disability (first 7 days not paid), and continues up to 2 years (104 weeks) maximum. You need a doctor's certificate.
consumer_tip: TTD benefit is particularly valuable for self-employed individuals and daily wage workers who have no employer sick pay. When buying PA insurance, check the TTD weekly amount — typically 1% of the sum assured per week. For a ₹20 lakh SA policy, TTD pays ₹20,000/week — close to a decent income replacement for most people.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Restoration Benefit
content: If the Sum Insured is exhausted on account of claims made during the policy year, the Company shall reinstate the Sum Insured once (or as specified in the Schedule) for the remaining policy year. The restored Sum Insured can be used for: (a) Any hospitalization for a completely different illness (in the case of basic restoration); (b) Any hospitalization including the same illness (in the case of unlimited restoration), as specified in the policy schedule. The restoration shall be applicable only when the Sum Insured has been completely exhausted and not for partial utilization.
plain_english: If you use up your entire sum insured in one hospital stay, the restoration benefit refills it — either once or unlimited times depending on your plan. Standard (basic) restoration: refilled SI can only be used for a DIFFERENT illness. Premium restoration (unlimited): refilled SI can be used for any illness, even the same one. Restoration only activates when SI is FULLY used up — not partially.
consumer_tip: Understand what type of restoration your policy has — basic or unlimited. Basic restoration is a useful feature but limited (cannot use it for follow-up treatment of the same illness). Unlimited restoration (like Niva Bupa ReAssure, Star Comprehensive) is far superior — especially for cancer, dialysis patients, or families with multiple members. The premium difference is usually modest.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV)
content: The Guaranteed Surrender Value (GSV) shall be the sum equal to the guaranteed surrender value factor multiplied by total premiums paid (excluding first year premium, service tax/GST, and rider premiums). The GSV shall be available after completion of at least 2 consecutive years of premium payment. The GSV factor shall be as specified in the policy schedule and shall not be less than 30% of total premiums paid (excluding first year) as mandated by IRDAI Surrender Value Regulations 2024.
plain_english: If you stop paying premiums after at least 2 years and want to exit the policy, you get the Guaranteed Surrender Value. This is calculated as a percentage of your total premiums (excluding first year premium and taxes). The minimum GSV is 30% of premiums paid from year 2 onwards. This amount increases as more premiums are paid and the policy approaches maturity.
consumer_tip: GSV is the minimum you will receive. The Special Surrender Value (SSV) may be higher — always ask your insurer to calculate both GSV and SSV before surrendering. If SSV is higher, you get SSV. Never surrender a policy in the first 3-5 years — the surrender value is very low compared to premiums paid.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Special Surrender Value (SSV)
content: The Special Surrender Value (SSV) shall be calculated as the present value of the Paid-Up Sum Assured plus the present value of accrued reversionary bonuses plus the present value of terminal bonus (if any), discounted at the rate notified by the insurer. The SSV shall not be less than the GSV at any point during the policy term. The insurer shall pay the higher of GSV and SSV to the policyholder on surrender.
plain_english: The SSV is a more sophisticated calculation that considers what your policy would eventually pay at maturity, discounted to today's value. It takes into account the bonuses already earned and the reduced coverage the policy would provide. SSV is usually higher than GSV, especially for policies held for many years. You always get the higher of the two.
consumer_tip: Ask the insurer to provide both GSV and SSV figures in writing before making a surrender decision. The SSV increases significantly after 10+ years of premium payment. Sometimes keeping the policy as paid-up (stopping premiums but not surrendering) gives better returns than surrendering, since the bonuses continue to accrue on the paid-up value.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Revival of Lapsed Policy
content: A policy which has lapsed due to non-payment of premium within the grace period may be revived during the lifetime of the Life Assured, within a period of 5 consecutive years from the date of the first unpaid premium and before the date of maturity, on submission of proof of continued insurability satisfactory to the Company and payment of all arrears of premium together with interest at such rate as may be determined by the Company from time to time. On revival, the policy shall be restored with all benefits from the date of revival.
plain_english: If your life policy has lapsed because you missed premium payments, you can revive it within 5 years. You need to: (1) Pay all the missed premiums with interest, and (2) Prove you are still in good health (medical tests may be required for high sum assured). Once revived, the policy is fully active again with all benefits restored from the revival date.
consumer_tip: Revival is almost always better than buying a new policy — because your age at inception was lower (younger), your premium stays the same. A new policy at your current age will be more expensive. Also, waiting periods restart from revival date, and the 12-month suicide clause restarts. Try to revive within 6 months of lapse to avoid medical tests.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Death Claim Settlement Process
content: Upon receipt of notification of death of the Life Assured, the Company shall: (a) issue claim forms within 3 working days of notification; (b) request all relevant documents within 15 days; (c) process and settle the claim within 30 days of receiving all required documents for non-investigated claims; (d) for claims requiring investigation (death within 3 years of policy commencement or suspicious circumstances), complete investigation within 90 days and settle within 120 days of claim notification. Interest at 2% above prevailing bank rate shall be payable on claims settled beyond 30 days.
plain_english: When the policyholder dies, the nominee notifies the insurer. The insurer must act quickly: send claim forms within 3 days, ask for documents within 15 days, and settle within 30 days of getting complete documents. If the death happened within 3 years of policy start and the insurer investigates, they get up to 120 days total. If they take longer than 30 days for non-investigated claims, they must pay interest on the delayed amount.
consumer_tip: Submit all documents together — do not send them in pieces. The 30-day clock starts only when ALL documents are submitted. Keep the policy document, premium receipts, and nominee's bank details ready. If the insurer asks for additional documents, respond within 7 days to avoid further delays. If settlement exceeds 30 days, claim interest.

policy_name: Standard ULIP — Fund Switch and Premium Redirection
content: The Policyholder may switch the existing fund value from one fund to another fund offered under this policy, subject to: (a) first 4 switches per policy year being free of charge; (b) a switching charge of ₹100 per switch for switches beyond 4 per year; (c) minimum switch amount: 25% of fund value or ₹5,000 (whichever is higher). Premium Redirection: the Policyholder may redirect future premiums to different funds by written request at least 7 days before the next premium due date, at no charge.
plain_english: In a ULIP, you can move your money between different funds (equity, debt, balanced). The first 4 switches each year are free. Beyond that, each switch costs ₹100. You can also change where your future premiums go (premium redirection) — this is always free. Fund switching helps you manage risk — move from equity to debt when markets are high, and vice versa.
consumer_tip: Use fund switches strategically — do not switch on every market dip. The first 4 per year are free. Before retirement or when approaching maturity, gradually switch from equity funds to debt funds to protect your gains. Premium redirection is free and unlimited — use this to adjust your ongoing investment strategy without touching existing corpus.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Free Look Period (Detailed)
content: The Policyholder shall have the right to review the terms and conditions of this policy within 15 days from the date of receipt of the policy document (30 days for policies sold through distance mode including online, telephone, or electronic communication). If the Policyholder disagrees with any terms and conditions, the policy may be returned with reasons for objection. The Company shall refund the premium paid less: (a) proportionate risk premium for the period on cover; (b) stamp duty charges; (c) expenses on medical examination, if any. For ULIPs, refund shall be at the prevailing NAV of the units.
plain_english: You have 15 days (30 days for online policies) from receiving your policy document to read it thoroughly and return it if you are not satisfied. You get back almost all your money — only a small deduction for the days you were actually covered and stamp duty. For ULIPs, you get back the current value of your units (which may be slightly less or more than what you paid, depending on market movement in those few days).
consumer_tip: This is your safety net against mis-selling. READ the policy document as soon as you receive it. Compare what you were told by the agent with what the policy actually says. If ANYTHING is different — return it. Common mis-selling red flags: agent said 'guaranteed returns' for ULIP, agent said 'like FD', agent did not mention charges or lock-in period. Free look is your exit without penalty.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Exclusions (Comprehensive)
content: The following are excluded from the scope of this policy: (a) Death due to suicide within 12 months from the date of commencement of risk or date of revival — nominee entitled to 80% of premiums paid or surrender value (whichever is higher); (b) Death resulting from the Insured's participation in any criminal or illegal activity; (c) Death due to self-inflicted injury while sane or insane (within first year); (d) Death resulting from aviation other than as a fare-paying passenger in a commercially licensed aircraft. Note: After completion of 12 months from commencement, no exclusions apply — all causes of death are covered including suicide.
plain_english: Life insurance has very few exclusions: suicide within first year (family still gets 80% of premiums back), death during criminal activity, self-harm (first year only), and non-commercial aviation accidents. After the first year, essentially ALL causes of death are covered — including suicide, adventure sports, hazardous occupations, and even death abroad. This is why term insurance is one of the simplest financial products.
consumer_tip: After completing one year, your term insurance covers death from virtually any cause. Do not worry about complex exclusion lists like health insurance has. The only practical advice: do not lie in your proposal form about health, smoking, or occupation — if you die within 3 years and the insurer investigates and finds misrepresentation, they can reject the claim (Section 45). After 3 years, even misrepresentation cannot be grounds for rejection.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Depreciation Schedule for Parts
content: In the event of a claim for replacement of parts/accessories, the following depreciation shall be deducted from the cost of replacement parts: (a) Rubber, nylon, plastic parts, tyres and tubes, air bags, batteries: 50% depreciation; (b) Fibre glass components: 30% depreciation; (c) Glass parts: Nil depreciation; (d) Metal body parts: 0% for vehicles up to 6 months old, 5% for 6 months to 1 year, 10% for 1-2 years, 15% for 2-3 years, 25% for 3-5 years, 40% for 5-10 years, 50% for above 10 years. (e) Painting: actual cost with no depreciation for vehicles up to 2 years; 10% for 2-5 years.
plain_english: When your car is repaired after an accident, the insurer deducts depreciation on replaced parts based on the type of part and the age of the car. Plastic and rubber parts always get 50% depreciation (you pay half). Glass has zero depreciation. Metal parts depreciation increases with car age — from 0% for new cars to 50% for cars above 10 years old. This depreciation deduction can make your out-of-pocket cost significant on an older car.
consumer_tip: The depreciation deduction is the biggest reason people are surprised by their motor claim payout. On a 5-year-old car, if plastic bumpers, rubber seals, and a battery are replaced in addition to metal body panels — the depreciation deduction can be 30-50% of the total repair bill. This is exactly why zero depreciation add-on is valuable for new cars (0-5 years). For older cars, zero dep is usually not available.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — NCB Transfer and Portability
content: No Claim Bonus (NCB) is a discount on Own Damage premium earned for each claim-free year of insurance: 20% after 1 claim-free year, 25% after 2 years, 35% after 3 years, 45% after 4 years, and 50% after 5 or more consecutive claim-free years. NCB is the earned right of the policyholder and is transferable: (a) to a new vehicle purchased by the same owner; (b) to a different insurer at renewal; (c) on transfer of vehicle ownership, the NCB remains with the previous owner. NCB is forfeited if a claim is made during the policy year, resetting to 0%.
plain_english: NCB is a reward for not making claims — it saves you 20-50% on your OD premium. After 5 claim-free years, you get 50% off your OD premium. The NCB belongs to YOU, not your car. If you sell your car and buy a new one, the NCB transfers to your new car. If you switch insurers, the NCB transfers. If you make even one OD claim, your NCB resets to 0%.
consumer_tip: Before making a small claim, calculate whether it is worth losing your NCB. Example: if your OD premium is ₹15,000 and you have 50% NCB (saving ₹7,500/year), making a ₹5,000 claim means losing ₹7,500 in NCB — a net loss of ₹2,500. For small damages, it is often cheaper to pay out of pocket and preserve your NCB. Consider the NCB Protection add-on if you have high NCB.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Cashless Repair at Network Garage
content: The Company offers cashless repair facility at its network of authorized garages. The Insured shall: (a) intimate the Company/TPA within 24 hours of the incident; (b) submit claim form with damage photographs; (c) the Company shall appoint a surveyor within 24 hours; (d) upon surveyor's assessment, cashless authorization shall be issued to the network garage; (e) the Insured shall pay only the applicable deductible, depreciation on replaced parts, and any non-covered items; (f) repair quality is assured by the Company for 6 months from date of repair completion.
plain_english: With cashless repair at a network garage, the insurer pays the garage directly — you only pay the deductible (₹1,000-2,000), depreciation on parts, and any non-covered items. You need to inform the insurer within 24 hours of the accident, submit photos, and go to one of their network garages. The surveyor assesses damage, approves the repair estimate, and the garage does the work. There is a 6-month quality guarantee on repairs.
consumer_tip: Always prefer cashless at a network garage over reimbursement. Advantages: (1) You do not pay upfront, (2) No document submission hassle, (3) 6-month repair quality guarantee. To get the best experience: call the insurer immediately after the accident, take photos of damage from multiple angles, and go to the nearest network garage. Use the insurer's app to find network garages near you.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Third Party Liability Section
content: The Company hereby agrees to indemnify the Insured against all sums which the Insured shall become legally liable to pay as damages, including claimant's costs and expenses, in respect of: (a) death of or bodily injury to any person (including occupants carried for hire or reward) but excluding employees; (b) damage to property of any third party, up to the limit of ₹7.5 lakh per event. The Company shall also pay all costs and expenses incurred with its written consent. The liability under this section is unlimited for death and bodily injury claims.
plain_english: If your vehicle causes an accident injuring or killing someone, the insurer pays the compensation awarded by the Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal (MACT). For bodily injury and death, there is NO upper limit — the insurer pays whatever the court awards (can be ₹10 lakh, ₹50 lakh, or even crores). For damage to someone else's property (their car, shop, wall), the limit is ₹7.5 lakh per incident.
consumer_tip: Third-party insurance is compulsory under the Motor Vehicles Act — driving without it is a criminal offense. TP premium is fixed by IRDAI and is the same across all insurers. If you are involved in an accident causing third-party injury, immediately inform your insurer and cooperate with the investigation. Never admit liability at the accident scene — let the tribunal and insurer handle liability determination.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Personal Accident Owner-Driver Mandatory Cover
content: Compulsory Personal Accident cover for the owner-driver of the vehicle: sum insured of ₹15 lakh payable in the event of: (a) Accidental death: 100% of sum insured (₹15 lakh); (b) Permanent total disability: 100% of sum insured; (c) Permanent partial disability: as per IRDAI Schedule of Disabilities. This cover is available only when the vehicle is being driven by the registered owner named in the policy. The owner-driver may opt out of this cover only if they hold a separate standalone PA policy with sum insured of ₹15 lakh or more.
plain_english: Every motor insurance policy includes a mandatory ₹15 lakh PA cover for the vehicle owner when driving. If the owner dies in a vehicle accident, the nominee gets ₹15 lakh. If the owner suffers permanent disability, they receive compensation as per the disability schedule. This cover applies ONLY to the registered owner driving the vehicle — not to other authorized drivers.
consumer_tip: This ₹15 lakh CPA cover costs only ₹750/year for cars and ₹200/year for two-wheelers. You can opt out ONLY if you already have a standalone PA policy worth ₹15 lakh+. There is no reason to opt out — the premium is negligible and the cover is valuable. Note: this covers only the owner-driver — for paid driver or named driver coverage, you need a separate add-on.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Zero Depreciation Add-On (Detailed)
content: Under this add-on, the depreciation deduction on replacement parts as specified in the standard depreciation schedule shall be waived. The Company shall pay the full invoice cost of replacement parts without any depreciation deduction, subject to: (a) maximum 2 claims per policy year under this add-on; (b) compulsory deductible still applies; (c) rubber, nylon, and plastic parts payable at 50% under this add-on (not 100%); (d) vehicle age: available only for vehicles up to 5 years old at inception; (e) tyres and tubes: payable at 50% under this add-on; (f) consumables (engine oil, coolant, nuts/bolts) are NOT covered under zero depreciation — separate consumable add-on needed.
plain_english: Zero depreciation waives the age-based depreciation deduction on metal, fiberglass, and glass parts. Instead of paying 25-50% of replacement part cost out of pocket, you pay nothing (except the deductible). BUT: rubber and plastic parts are still at 50% (not zero), tyres at 50%, and consumables (oil, coolant) are not covered. You get a maximum of 2 claims per year with zero dep.
consumer_tip: Zero dep is most valuable for new cars (0-3 years) and luxury cars where parts are expensive. After the car is 5 years old, zero dep is usually unavailable. Even with zero dep, there are items you still pay: compulsory deductible (₹1,000-2,000), rubber/plastic at 50%, consumables. For comprehensive protection, consider adding both zero dep AND consumable cover add-ons.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Pre-existing Disease (PED) Definition (Detailed)
content: Pre-existing Disease means any condition, ailment, injury, or disease: (a) that is/are diagnosed by a physician within 48 months prior to the effective date of the policy issued by the insurer or its reinstatement; OR (b) for which medical advice or treatment was recommended by, or received from, a physician within 48 months prior to the effective date of the policy; OR (c) which was known to the insured at the time of proposal, irrespective of whether medical advice was sought. The determination of whether a condition is pre-existing shall be based on medical records, diagnostic reports, and physician certifications available at the time of claim assessment.
plain_english: A pre-existing disease (PED) is any health condition you had or knew about in the 4 years before buying the policy. This includes: conditions diagnosed by a doctor, conditions for which you received treatment or advice, and conditions you were aware of even if you did not seek treatment. If you had knee pain for years but never saw a doctor — and your insurer can prove you knew about it — it can be classified as PED.
consumer_tip: The most important thing: DISCLOSE everything in your proposal form. If you mention your conditions upfront, the insurer can either accept with exclusion or charge higher premium — but they cannot reject your claim later for non-disclosure. The PED waiting period (4 years, reducing to 3 years from 2025) starts from Day 1 of your policy. After that, PED is fully covered. After 8 years (moratorium), insurer cannot challenge any condition as PED.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — AYUSH Treatment Coverage
content: The Company shall provide coverage for hospitalization treatment under Ayurveda, Yoga and Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy (AYUSH) systems of medicine, provided: (a) the treatment is taken in a government hospital or a hospital recognized by the Central/State Government or accredited by the Quality Council of India/NABH; (b) the treatment is administered by a qualified AYUSH practitioner registered with the respective state board; (c) the hospitalization is for a minimum of 24 hours (or qualifies as day-care procedure); (d) the maximum coverage under this benefit shall be equal to the sum insured (no separate sub-limit effective from 2024).
plain_english: Your health insurance covers treatment under Indian traditional medicine systems: Ayurveda, Yoga, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy. The treatment must be at a recognized hospital with a registered practitioner. From 2024, AYUSH coverage is at par with allopathic treatment — up to your full sum insured without a separate sub-limit. Panchakarma therapy in an Ayurvedic hospital is covered if it requires hospitalization.
consumer_tip: AYUSH coverage is often underutilized. If you prefer Ayurvedic or Homeopathic treatment for chronic conditions like arthritis, digestive disorders, or skin conditions — check if the hospital is NABH-accredited or government-recognized. Ensure the practitioner is registered with the state AYUSH board. Keep all prescriptions and treatment records — same documentation standard as allopathic treatment applies.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Modern Treatment Methods Coverage
content: The Company shall cover the following modern treatment methods when prescribed by the treating specialist: (a) Robotic surgery (including da Vinci robotic system); (b) Stem cell therapy (for IRDAI-approved indications); (c) Immunotherapy (for cancer treatment); (d) Gene therapy (for approved genetic conditions); (e) Balloon sinuplasty; (f) Deep brain stimulation; (g) Oral chemotherapy; (h) Intra-vitreal injections; (i) Proton beam therapy; (j) HIFU (High Intensity Focused Ultrasound); (k) Uterine artery embolization. The coverage shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this policy including the sum insured and applicable sub-limits.
plain_english: Your health insurance must cover advanced medical treatments including robotic surgery, stem cell therapy, immunotherapy for cancer, and other modern procedures. The insurer cannot refuse to cover a treatment just because it uses advanced technology. If your doctor prescribes robotic surgery for prostate cancer, the insurer must cover the full cost including the robot usage charges — they cannot limit it to what an open surgery would have cost.
consumer_tip: If your insurer rejects a claim for robotic surgery citing it as 'not covered' or limits payout to 'equivalent open surgery cost' — this violates IRDAI guidelines. File a grievance citing the IRDAI circular on modern treatment methods. Robotic surgery typically costs ₹2-4 lakh more than traditional surgery but offers faster recovery. Your health insurance must cover the full cost.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Maternity Coverage (Detailed Terms)
content: Maternity Benefit shall cover: (a) Normal delivery and caesarean section (C-section) up to the maternity sub-limit specified in the Schedule; (b) Pre-natal expenses: consultations, diagnostics, and medications for 30 days prior to delivery date; (c) Post-natal expenses: mother and newborn care for 60 days after delivery date; (d) Ectopic pregnancy: covered without waiting period (treated as emergency); (e) Lawful medical termination of pregnancy: covered subject to maternity sub-limit; (f) Newborn coverage from birth: covered under the policy for first 90 days from date of birth without separate enrollment. Maternity benefit is subject to a waiting period of [24/36/48] months from the date of commencement of the first policy with the insurer.
plain_english: Maternity coverage pays for delivery (normal or C-section), pre-natal care (30 days before), post-natal care (60 days after), and newborn care from birth. There is a waiting period (2-4 years depending on the plan) before maternity coverage activates. Ectopic pregnancy is treated as a medical emergency with no waiting period. The newborn is automatically covered for 90 days from birth — you do not need to separately enroll the baby during that period.
consumer_tip: Plan ahead: buy health insurance with maternity cover 2-4 years before you plan to have a baby to serve the waiting period. Check the maternity sub-limit carefully — it varies from ₹25,000 to ₹1 lakh+ depending on the plan. C-section typically costs ₹50,000-₹2 lakh depending on the city and hospital — ensure your sub-limit is adequate. Also check if the newborn is automatically covered for the first 90 days — this saves significant expense for NICU stays.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Room Rent Proportionate Deduction (Worked Example)
content: Example: Policy room rent limit: ₹5,000/day. Patient admitted to room costing ₹10,000/day (ratio: 50%). Total hospital bill: Room charges: ₹50,000 (5 days x ₹10,000). Surgeon fees: ₹80,000. Nursing: ₹15,000. ICU: ₹40,000. Medicines: ₹25,000. Diagnostics: ₹20,000. Total: ₹2,30,000. Proportionate deduction applied at 50% ratio to ALL charges: Admissible amount: ₹1,15,000 (50% of ₹2,30,000). Patient pays: ₹1,15,000 out of pocket. If patient had chosen ₹5,000/day room: Full ₹2,30,000 would be covered (within sum insured).
plain_english: This example shows the devastating impact of room rent proportionate deduction. By choosing a room costing double the policy limit, the patient does not just lose ₹25,000 in room rent difference — they lose ₹1,15,000 because ALL charges (surgeon, ICU, medicines, diagnostics) are also cut by 50%. The total out-of-pocket cost is ₹1,15,000 instead of ₹0.
consumer_tip: This is the single most expensive mistake in health insurance claims. ALWAYS choose a room within your policy limit. If no lower room is available, get a letter from the hospital admin confirming unavailability — this can be used to argue against proportionate deduction. Better yet: buy a policy without room rent sub-limits. The slightly higher premium saves you lakhs during a major claim.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Moratorium Period (8 Years)
content: After completion of 8 continuous years under this policy (including portability credit from previous insurer), no claim shall be contestable on the ground that a pre-existing disease or condition was not disclosed at the time of taking the policy or any renewal thereof. This moratorium shall apply to all conditions, whether or not disclosed, and whether or not known to the insured at the time of proposal. The only exception to this moratorium is provable fraud, which must be established by the insurer through documentary evidence in a court of competent jurisdiction.
plain_english: After 8 continuous years of health insurance coverage (including years with a previous insurer if you ported), the insurer CANNOT reject any claim based on pre-existing disease non-disclosure. Even if you deliberately hid a condition when buying the policy, after 8 years it cannot be used against you. The only exception is if the insurer proves intentional fraud in a court — which is a very high legal bar.
consumer_tip: The 8-year moratorium is your strongest consumer protection in health insurance. After 8 continuous years, you have near-absolute claim protection. This is why continuous coverage is critical — never let your policy lapse, as the moratorium clock resets. When porting to a new insurer, ensure they credit your previous coverage years toward the moratorium. Also note: there is a 5-year continuous coverage rule after which the PED waiting period (4 years) is deemed served regardless.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Mental Health Coverage (Mandatory)
content: In compliance with Section 21(4) of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and IRDAI directives, the Company shall cover treatment of mental illness on the same basis as physical illness. Covered conditions include but are not limited to: depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, anxiety disorders, obsessive-compulsive disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, eating disorders, substance abuse disorders (when under psychiatric supervision), and any other condition classified as mental illness under the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017. Coverage includes: inpatient psychiatric hospitalization, psychiatric medications, psychotherapy during hospitalization, and rehabilitation.
plain_english: Mental illness is covered by your health insurance — this is your legal right under the Mental Healthcare Act. Whether it is depression requiring hospitalization, schizophrenia treatment, severe anxiety, or substance abuse de-addiction — the insurer must cover it just like they cover heart disease or cancer. The insurer cannot charge extra premium for mental health history or exclude mental illness from your policy.
consumer_tip: If your health insurer rejects a mental health claim, you have an extremely strong legal position. Cite: (1) Mental Healthcare Act 2017, Section 21(4), (2) IRDAI circular mandating mental health coverage. File with IRDAI Bima Bharosa immediately. Consumer forums have consistently ruled against insurers who reject mental health claims. OPD mental health (therapy sessions, psychiatrist consultations) may or may not be covered — check if your policy has OPD benefit.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Cancer Treatment Coverage
content: The Company shall cover all medically necessary treatment for cancer including: (a) Chemotherapy (intravenous and oral); (b) Radiation therapy (external beam, brachytherapy, proton beam); (c) Immunotherapy and targeted therapy; (d) Hormonal therapy; (e) Bone marrow transplant/stem cell transplant; (f) Surgical procedures including robotic surgery; (g) PET scan and cancer diagnostics; (h) Prosthetics after cancer surgery (breast prosthesis after mastectomy, limb prosthesis); (i) Post-cancer rehabilitation and palliative care. Cancer claims shall not be subject to any disease-specific sub-limit lower than 50% of the sum insured.
plain_english: Health insurance must cover comprehensive cancer treatment: surgery, chemotherapy, radiation, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, bone marrow transplant, and even robotic surgery for cancer. Post-cancer prosthetics (like breast prosthesis after mastectomy) and rehabilitation are also covered. The insurer cannot set a separate cancer sub-limit lower than 50% of your sum insured — if your SI is ₹20 lakh, cancer treatment must be covered for at least ₹10 lakh.
consumer_tip: Cancer treatment can cost ₹5-25 lakh depending on the type and stage. Ensure your health insurance sum insured is adequate. Consider: a super top-up on top of your base policy to enhance cancer coverage. Oral chemotherapy at home is also covered — you do not need to be hospitalized for each chemo cycle. If your insurer tries to reject cancer drugs as 'investigational' or 'not proven', get your oncologist to certify medical necessity.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Trip Cancellation (Detailed Clauses)
content: The Company shall reimburse the Insured for unused, non-refundable, pre-paid trip expenses for trip cancellation due to: (a) sudden serious illness/injury requiring hospitalization of the Insured, Insured's spouse, or dependent child within 30 days of departure; (b) death of immediate family member; (c) natural disaster making destination officially declared uninhabitable; (d) visa denial with documented proof of rejection; (e) jury duty or compulsory government service with written orders. Maximum reimbursement: actual non-refundable expenses or 100% of trip cost (whichever is lower). Exclusions: change of mind, financial inability, fear of travel, employer pressure, or any other reason within the Insured's control.
plain_english: Trip cancellation insurance reimburses non-refundable trip costs (flights, hotels, tours) when you cannot travel due to specific documented reasons: serious illness, family death, natural disaster, or visa rejection. It does NOT cover: deciding not to go, being afraid to travel, work pressure, or any personal reason. You must prove the cancellation reason with documentation — hospital certificate, death certificate, embassy rejection letter.
consumer_tip: Book refundable travel wherever possible — it is often cheaper than the trip cancellation premium. For non-refundable bookings: buy travel insurance immediately after booking (not at the last minute). If you need to cancel: get documentation FIRST (doctor's certificate, etc.) before cancelling bookings. The insurer will ask for proof that the reason was genuine and unforeseeable.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Medical Evacuation and Repatriation
content: The Company shall cover: (a) Emergency medical evacuation: transportation of the Insured to the nearest adequate medical facility when local treatment is inadequate — by air ambulance, commercial flight (business class if medical necessity), or ground ambulance; (b) Repatriation to India: when the Insured is medically stable, transportation back to India including medical escort if required; (c) Repatriation of mortal remains: transportation of deceased Insured's body to India including embalming, casket, and customs documentation; (d) Accompaniment benefit: economy class round-trip ticket for one family member to travel to the Insured's location in case of hospitalization exceeding 7 days. Maximum coverage: USD 100,000 or as specified in the Schedule.
plain_english: If you fall seriously ill abroad and the local hospital cannot treat you adequately, travel insurance covers emergency evacuation to a better hospital (by air ambulance if needed). Once stable, it covers your return to India with medical escort. If you die abroad, it covers transportation of your body back to India. If you are hospitalized for more than 7 days, it even covers a family member's flight ticket to be with you.
consumer_tip: Medical evacuation is the most expensive component of travel insurance and the most critical. An air ambulance from Europe to India can cost ₹30-50 lakh. Without travel insurance, your family would bear this cost. Always buy travel insurance with at least USD 100,000 medical evacuation coverage. For adventure travel or trekking at altitude, ensure evacuation from remote locations (helicopter rescue) is explicitly covered.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Flight Delay and Missed Connection
content: The Company shall provide the following benefits in the event of flight delay: (a) Flight delay of 6 hours or more at the point of departure: reimbursement of reasonable expenses for meals, refreshments, and hotel accommodation up to [₹5,000/USD 100] per 6-hour delay block; (b) Flight delay of 12 hours or more: additional fixed benefit of [₹10,000/USD 200]; (c) Missed connecting flight due to delay of incoming flight: reimbursement of additional travel costs to reach the destination by the next available route, up to [₹25,000/USD 500]; (d) Trip curtailment: if the delayed flight results in loss of 24 hours or more of the planned trip, reimbursement of proportionate unused tour/accommodation costs.
plain_english: If your flight is delayed by 6+ hours, travel insurance reimburses meals and hotel costs during the wait. A delay of 12+ hours gives an additional fixed compensation. If you miss a connecting flight because your first flight was delayed, the additional cost to rebook and reach your destination is covered. If the delay eats into your trip significantly (24+ hours lost), you can claim proportionate refund of unused tour costs.
consumer_tip: Keep all receipts during flight delays — meal bills, hotel receipts, and rebooking charges. Get written confirmation from the airline of the delay and its duration. If the airline provides meals/accommodation, you cannot double-claim from insurance. If the airline cancels your flight (not just delays), the airline bears responsibility under their contract of carriage — file with the airline first, then insurance for any gaps.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Organ Donor Expenses Coverage
content: The Company shall cover all medical expenses of the organ donor during the course of organ harvesting and related procedures, subject to the Insured Person's Sum Insured. Coverage includes: (a) Pre-operative assessment and testing of the organ donor; (b) Surgical expenses for organ harvesting; (c) Hospitalization of the organ donor including ICU and ward charges; (d) Post-operative care and follow-up of the organ donor for up to 30 days post-surgery; (e) Organ preservation and transportation costs. The organ donor need not be a policyholder under this or any insurance policy. The coverage shall be in addition to the recipient's transplant surgery coverage.
plain_english: When you need an organ transplant and a living donor donates an organ (like a kidney), all the donor's medical expenses are covered by YOUR health insurance. This includes the donor's testing, surgery, hospitalization, and 30-day follow-up. The donor does not need their own insurance. These expenses come from your sum insured but are in addition to your own transplant surgery costs.
consumer_tip: This is a critical benefit that many policyholders are not aware of. If you need a kidney transplant and a family member donates a kidney, the donor's entire medical bill (typically ₹2-4 lakh) is covered by your health insurance. Ensure your sum insured is adequate to cover both your transplant and the donor's expenses. Some policies also cover organ transportation costs if the donor is in a different city.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Guaranteed Renewal and Non-Cancellation
content: This policy is guaranteed renewable for life. The Company shall not cancel, refuse to renew, or impose new exclusions on this policy: (a) on account of claims made during the policy year; (b) on account of the Insured Person's deterioration in health; (c) on account of the Insured Person's advancing age. The Company may revise premium rates applicable to the entire portfolio of policyholders in the same age band and product category, subject to IRDAI approval. No individual-specific premium increase shall be levied. The Company may discontinue the product — in which case, the policyholder shall be migrated to an equivalent or better product at the same or lower premium.
plain_english: Your health insurance is guaranteed renewable for life. The insurer cannot cancel your policy or refuse renewal because you made claims or because your health has worsened. They can increase premiums — but only across the board for everyone in your age group, not specifically for you because you claimed. If the insurer discontinues your product, they must migrate you to an equivalent product.
consumer_tip: This is one of the most important consumer protections in health insurance. Never worry about 'claiming too much' — your policy cannot be cancelled for making claims. If the insurer tries to refuse renewal, report to IRDAI immediately — this is a serious regulatory violation. When premiums increase at renewal, it should be a portfolio-wide increase, not targeted at you individually. Ask the insurer to confirm the increase is applicable to all policyholders in your age band.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Co-Payment Clause
content: Co-payment means a cost sharing requirement under this policy that provides that the Insured Person will bear a specified percentage of the admissible claim amount. The co-payment percentage applicable to this policy is specified in the Schedule of Benefits. Co-payment shall be applied after all other deductions (sub-limits, non-medical items, non-payable items) have been computed. The Company shall pay the claim amount minus the co-payment percentage. Co-payment for senior citizens (age 60+) shall not exceed 20% as per IRDAI guidelines.
plain_english: Co-payment means you share a percentage of every claim with the insurer. If your policy has a 10% co-pay and your approved claim is ₹5 lakh, the insurer pays ₹4.5 lakh and you pay ₹50,000. Co-pay is applied AFTER all other deductions — so it is calculated on the net admissible amount, not the total bill. For senior citizens, the maximum co-pay allowed is 20%.
consumer_tip: Co-pay reduces your premium but increases your out-of-pocket cost during claims. A 10% co-pay on a ₹10 lakh claim means you pay ₹1 lakh from your pocket. For young families, choose a policy with 0% co-pay (slightly higher premium but worth it). For senior citizen policies, 10-20% co-pay is common and helps keep premiums affordable. Always factor co-pay into your insurance planning — it is a significant cost during large claims.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Cumulative Bonus / No Claim Bonus
content: For each claim-free year, the Sum Insured shall be increased by [5-10]% of the base Sum Insured, subject to a maximum cumulative increase of [50-100]% of the base Sum Insured over the policy tenure. In the event of a claim during a policy year: (a) Basic NCB: the cumulative bonus is reduced by one step (not reset to zero); (b) Super NCB (if applicable): the cumulative bonus is retained even after one claim per year. The cumulative bonus shall be transferable to a new insurer in the event of portability, subject to verification of claim-free status from the previous insurer.
plain_english: Health insurance NCB works differently from motor NCB. Instead of giving you a premium discount, it increases your sum insured. Each claim-free year adds 5-10% to your SI. After 10 claim-free years, your SI could double. If you make a claim, you lose one step of bonus (not the entire bonus). When you port to a new insurer, your cumulative bonus transfers with you.
consumer_tip: Health NCB is extremely valuable over the long term. If you start with ₹10 lakh SI and get 10% NCB per year, after 10 claim-free years your effective SI is ₹20 lakh — without paying any additional premium. This is why health insurance becomes more valuable over time. Avoid making small claims (₹10,000-20,000) that would cost you a year's NCB increment. Save your insurance for significant medical events.
