policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Restoration Benefit (Detailed)
content: If the Sum Insured is exhausted during the policy year, the Sum Insured shall be reinstated as follows: (a) Basic Restoration: Sum Insured reinstated once per year for a DIFFERENT illness/condition than the one that exhausted the original SI; (b) Unlimited Restoration: Sum Insured reinstated for ANY illness (including the same illness) — available on select premium plans; (c) The restoration benefit is available for each insured member individually under a family floater; (d) Restoration activates only when the original Sum Insured is 100% exhausted (not for partial utilization). The reinstated Sum Insured is equal to the base Sum Insured (excluding any cumulative bonus).
plain_english: If you exhaust your entire sum insured in one claim, the restoration benefit refills it so you still have coverage for the rest of the year. Basic restoration: the refilled SI can only be used for a different illness. Unlimited restoration: the refilled SI can be used for any illness, including more treatment for the same condition. Restoration activates only when SI is fully used up, not when partially used.
consumer_tip: Understand which type of restoration your policy has — basic vs unlimited. For a family floater with 4 members: if one member exhausts the entire SI, basic restoration ensures other family members still have coverage (for different illnesses). Unlimited restoration is far superior — especially for cancer patients who may need multiple rounds of treatment costing more than one round of SI. Check if your policy restores the base SI or the enhanced SI (with cumulative bonus).

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Emergency Ambulance Coverage
content: The Company shall reimburse the cost of ambulance services for transportation of the Insured Person to the nearest hospital with adequate medical facilities: (a) Road ambulance: covered at actual cost up to ₹5,000 per hospitalization event or as specified in the Schedule; (b) Air ambulance: covered up to 10% of the Sum Insured or ₹5 lakh (whichever is lower) when ground transportation is not feasible due to terrain, distance, or medical urgency; (c) Government ambulance services (108/102): fully reimbursed at actual; (d) Inter-hospital ambulance transfer: covered when transfer is medically necessary for specialized treatment. Dead body transportation from hospital to residence or cremation ground is covered up to ₹10,000.
plain_english: Your health insurance covers ambulance costs — both road ambulance (up to ₹5,000 per event) and air ambulance (up to 10% of SI or ₹5 lakh). Government 108 ambulance charges are fully covered. If you need to be transferred from one hospital to another for better treatment, that ambulance is also covered. Even the cost of transporting a deceased family member from hospital to home is covered up to ₹10,000.
consumer_tip: In an emergency, call 108 (government ambulance) or the nearest private ambulance — do not worry about whether it is covered. Keep the ambulance receipt for reimbursement. For air ambulance (helicopter/air evacuation), this is typically needed for accidents in remote areas or mountain trekking injuries. Ensure your policy has air ambulance coverage if you travel frequently to remote areas. The ₹5,000 road ambulance limit may seem low — some premium policies offer higher limits or uncapped ambulance coverage.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Nomination and Assignment (Detailed Comparison)
content: Nomination (Section 39, Insurance Act): designates who receives the death benefit. Beneficial nominee (spouse, children, parents) is absolute owner. Non-beneficial nominee holds money as trustee for legal heirs. Assignment (Section 38): transfers ownership of the policy itself. Absolute assignment: complete transfer of all rights. Conditional assignment: ownership reverts on condition (e.g., loan repaid). Key difference: nomination takes effect only on death; assignment takes effect immediately during the policyholder's lifetime.
plain_english: Nomination and assignment are different legal concepts. Nomination is like naming someone to receive your mail — it only matters after your death. Assignment is transferring the policy itself to someone else during your lifetime. If your spouse is a nominee, they get the money when you die. If you assign the policy to a bank, the bank owns the policy rights now (during your lifetime) — they receive the payout until the loan is repaid.
consumer_tip: For most families: nominate your spouse and children as beneficial nominees — this gives them absolute ownership of the death benefit, bypassing any will disputes. Only assign your policy if required for a loan. Always use CONDITIONAL assignment for loans — so the policy reverts to you on loan repayment. After a home loan is repaid, verify that the bank has released the assignment — many banks forget this step.

policy_name: Standard ULIP — Lock-in Period and Discontinuance Charges
content: The minimum lock-in period for ULIPs shall be 5 years from the date of commencement of the policy. During the lock-in period: (a) Surrender/withdrawal of fund value is not permitted; (b) If premiums are discontinued during lock-in: the fund value is moved to a Discontinued Policy Fund earning minimum 4% p.a., and the fund value (minus discontinuance charges) is paid out on completion of the lock-in period. Discontinuance charges: Year 1: lower of ₹3,000 or 6% of fund value; Year 2: ₹2,500 or 4%; Year 3: ₹2,000 or 3%; Year 4: ₹1,500 or 2%; Year 5 onwards: Nil.
plain_english: You cannot withdraw from a ULIP for the first 5 years — this is a mandatory lock-in. If you stop paying premiums during this period, your money sits in a low-return fund (4% p.a.) and is returned to you after the 5-year lock-in ends, minus a discontinuance charge. The charge decreases each year — from ₹3,000 in year 1 to zero after year 5.
consumer_tip: The 5-year lock-in is why ULIPs should never be compared to mutual funds for short-term goals. If you cannot commit to 5 years of premium payment, do not buy a ULIP. After 5 years, you can surrender with zero charges and get your full fund value. During lock-in, if you stop premiums, your money earns only 4% — much less than keeping it invested in the market-linked fund. Stick with premium payments for at least 5 years.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Engine Protect Add-On
content: Subject to payment of additional premium, the Company agrees to cover loss or damage to the engine, gear box, and transmission resulting from: (a) Water ingression (hydrostatic lock) due to driving through waterlogged areas; (b) Lubricant leakage leading to engine seizure; (c) Damage to engine components due to entry of foreign matter. This add-on covers: engine repair/replacement costs including labor. Exclusions: (i) Normal wear and tear of engine; (ii) Damage due to lack of routine maintenance; (iii) Consequential loss beyond engine repair/replacement. Available only for vehicles up to 7 years old.
plain_english: The Engine Protect add-on covers your engine damage from driving through flooded roads (hydrostatic lock), which the standard policy does NOT cover. It also covers engine seizure from oil leaks and damage from foreign matter entering the engine. Without this add-on, engine damage from waterlogging is entirely your expense — and engine replacement can cost ₹50,000 to ₹5 lakh depending on the car.
consumer_tip: If you live in a city prone to flooding (Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Bengaluru), the Engine Protect add-on is essential. Many car owners learn this the hard way during monsoon — driving through a waterlogged road, engine hydrolocks, and the standard policy does not cover it. Engine replacement on a mid-range car can cost ₹1.5-3 lakh. The Engine Protect add-on premium is typically ₹500-2,000 — a small price for critical protection.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Return to Invoice (RTI) Add-On
content: Subject to payment of additional premium, in the event of Total Loss or Constructive Total Loss or Theft of the insured vehicle, the Company shall pay the difference between the ex-showroom invoice value of the vehicle (as per original purchase invoice) and the Insured's Declared Value (IDV) payable under the standard policy. Total payout: IDV + RTI amount = ex-showroom invoice value. Conditions: (a) Available for vehicles up to 5 years old; (b) Maximum 1 claim under this add-on per policy period; (c) Registration, road tax, and accessories value are NOT included in RTI — only ex-showroom price.
plain_english: With standard insurance, in a total loss or theft, you get the IDV (depreciated value). A 3-year-old car bought for ₹10 lakh might have an IDV of ₹7 lakh — you lose ₹3 lakh. The RTI add-on pays the gap: you get the full original invoice price (₹10 lakh in this case). This means zero financial loss on the car itself in case of total loss or theft.
consumer_tip: RTI is most valuable for new and expensive cars where the gap between invoice and IDV is large. For a ₹15 lakh car after 3 years: IDV might be ₹10.5 lakh, RTI fills the ₹4.5 lakh gap. The RTI add-on costs approximately 5-8% of OD premium — a small price to protect ₹4.5 lakh. Note: RTI does not cover registration fees, road tax, or accessories — only the ex-showroom price. For accessories, get a separate declared accessories cover.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Consumable Cover Add-On
content: Subject to payment of additional premium, the Company shall reimburse the cost of consumable items used during repair of the insured vehicle following an accident covered under this policy. Consumable items covered: engine oil, gear box oil, coolant, brake oil, AC gas/refrigerant, grease, washers, clips, nuts and bolts, screws, bearings, seals and gaskets, and similar consumable parts that are not covered under the standard policy. Maximum coverage per claim: actual cost or 5% of the total claim amount (whichever is lower).
plain_english: When your car is repaired after an accident, the standard policy does not cover consumable items like engine oil, brake fluid, coolant, AC gas, nuts and bolts, seals, and bearings. You pay for these out of pocket. The consumable cover add-on reimburses these costs. For a typical repair: consumables can add ₹2,000-10,000 depending on the extent of repair.
consumer_tip: Consumable cover is one of the most underrated motor add-ons. During major repairs, the consumable bill (oil, coolant, AC gas, seals, bearings) can be ₹5,000-15,000 — none of which is covered by the standard policy or even zero depreciation. The add-on costs approximately ₹300-800 per year. Combined with zero dep and engine protect, you get near-complete motor claim coverage with minimal out-of-pocket costs.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Super Top-Up Deductible Mechanism
content: This Super Top-Up Health Insurance Policy provides coverage for medical expenses that exceed the Deductible Amount specified in the Schedule. The Deductible is the threshold amount below which the Company has no liability. The Deductible operates on an aggregate basis across all claims during the policy year (not per claim). Example: If Deductible = ₹5 lakh and total claims in the year = ₹8 lakh, Company pays ₹3 lakh (₹8 lakh minus ₹5 lakh). The Deductible can be met by: (a) the Insured's own base health policy claims; (b) employer group health insurance claims; (c) out-of-pocket medical expenses.
plain_english: A super top-up is like a second layer of health insurance that kicks in after your base coverage is exhausted. You choose a deductible (say ₹5 lakh) matching your base policy sum insured. If total medical bills in a year exceed ₹5 lakh, the super top-up pays everything above ₹5 lakh up to its own sum insured. The deductible is aggregate per year — so multiple smaller claims can also breach the deductible.
consumer_tip: Super top-up is the most cost-effective way to enhance health coverage. A ₹50 lakh super top-up with ₹5 lakh deductible costs roughly ₹3,000-5,000/year for a 35-year-old — compared to ₹15,000-25,000 for a comprehensive ₹50 lakh policy. If you already have ₹5 lakh employer group coverage, add a ₹50 lakh super top-up with ₹5 lakh deductible — you are covered for ₹55 lakh total at minimal cost.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Subrogation Rights
content: The Insured Person shall take all reasonable steps to preserve and exercise their right of recovery against any third party. The Company shall be entitled to subrogation rights — the right to recover from any third party who is legally liable for the Insured Person's medical expenses. Upon settlement of a claim, the Insured Person shall transfer to the Company all rights of recovery against such third party. Any amount recovered through subrogation shall be applied: first to reimburse the Company, and any excess shall be paid to the Insured Person.
plain_english: If someone else is legally responsible for your injury (e.g., a road accident caused by another driver), your health insurer pays your medical bills but then has the right to recover that money from the person who caused the injury. You must cooperate with your insurer in this recovery process. If the insurer recovers more than they paid, the excess goes to you.
consumer_tip: This clause mainly applies to accident cases where a third party is at fault. Your health insurer pays your hospital bills immediately (cashless or reimbursement) and then pursues the at-fault party for recovery. You do not need to wait for the third party to pay — your health insurance works immediately. Keep the FIR copy and police report — your insurer may need them for subrogation.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Grace Period for Premium Payment
content: A grace period of 30 days from the premium due date shall be allowed for payment of each renewal premium. During the grace period, the policy shall remain in force with full coverage. If the premium is not paid within the grace period, the policy shall lapse. A lapsed policy can be revived within [90/180] days of lapse by paying the outstanding premium. Claims arising during the grace period are payable — the outstanding premium shall be deducted from the claim amount. Claims during lapsed period are NOT payable.
plain_english: You have 30 extra days after your premium due date to pay without losing coverage. During these 30 days, if you are hospitalized, the claim is still valid — the insurer will deduct the unpaid premium from the claim payout. If you do not pay within 30 days, the policy lapses and you have no coverage. You can revive a lapsed policy within 90-180 days by paying the premium, but claims during the lapse period are not covered.
consumer_tip: Set up auto-debit for your health insurance premium — never let it lapse. If your policy lapses and you revive it, some insurers may impose fresh waiting periods or require medical underwriting again. A 30-day gap in coverage can be very risky — if you are hospitalized during the lapse period, you are entirely uninsured. If you are facing financial difficulty, contact the insurer before the grace period ends — some offer premium instalment options.

policy_name: Standard Term Insurance — Accidental Death Benefit Rider
content: Accidental Death Benefit Rider provides an additional sum assured over and above the base policy sum assured in the event of death of the Life Assured due to an accident. The Rider Sum Assured shall be equal to the Base Sum Assured (effectively doubling the death benefit on accidental death). Accident is defined as a sudden, unforeseen, external, and violent event causing bodily injury which independently of any other cause results in death within 180 days of such event. Exclusions: suicide, self-inflicted injury, war, nuclear, participation in criminal activity, death under influence of alcohol/drugs (with blood alcohol above legal limit).
plain_english: This rider doubles your death benefit if you die in an accident. If your term plan sum assured is ₹1 crore, the accidental death rider adds another ₹1 crore — your family gets ₹2 crore on accidental death. The death must occur within 180 days of the accident and must be caused solely by the accident. Standard exclusions apply: drunk driving, criminal activity, war.
consumer_tip: The accidental death rider costs very little (₹300-500 per year for ₹1 crore rider) because accidental deaths are statistically rare. It is worth adding for the marginal cost. However, do not over-rely on AD riders — ensure your base term sum assured is adequate for your family's needs regardless of death cause. Most deaths in India are from illness (heart disease, cancer), not accidents — so the base policy is more important than the rider.

policy_name: Standard Term Insurance — Critical Illness Rider
content: Critical Illness Rider provides a lump sum benefit equal to the CI Rider Sum Assured upon first diagnosis of any of the following critical illnesses: (1) Cancer of specified severity; (2) First heart attack — of specified severity with troponin elevation; (3) Coronary artery bypass surgery; (4) Stroke with permanent neurological deficit; (5) Kidney failure requiring regular dialysis; (6) Major organ transplant; (7) Paralysis of limbs; (8) Multiple sclerosis; (9) Major burns; (10) Blindness. The CI benefit is payable subject to a 30-day survival period from the date of diagnosis. The CI benefit is paid in addition to (and not in lieu of) the base policy death benefit.
plain_english: The CI rider pays a lump sum when you are diagnosed with a listed critical illness. If your CI rider is ₹25 lakh and you are diagnosed with cancer, you get ₹25 lakh immediately (after surviving 30 days from diagnosis). This money is for treatment costs, income replacement, and lifestyle modifications. Your base term insurance continues — if you die later, your family still gets the full death benefit.
consumer_tip: CI riders are valuable but have strict definitions. For example: 'cancer of specified severity' excludes early-stage cancers like Stage 0/carcinoma in situ, some skin cancers, and early thyroid cancers. 'Heart attack' requires specific troponin levels and ECG findings. Read the definitions carefully. The CI lump sum is most useful as income replacement during recovery — it gives you money when you cannot work. Consider a CI rider of at least 5-10x your monthly income.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Bariatric Surgery Coverage
content: Bariatric surgery (weight loss surgery) shall be covered under this policy subject to the following conditions: (a) The Insured Person's BMI exceeds 35 (morbid obesity) OR BMI exceeds 30 with at least one obesity-related co-morbidity (Type 2 diabetes, severe sleep apnea, obesity-related heart disease); (b) The Insured Person has completed a documented 6-month supervised weight loss program that was unsuccessful; (c) The surgery is recommended by a qualified bariatric surgeon; (d) A waiting period of 24 months from policy inception applies. Coverage includes: gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy, adjustable gastric banding, and post-surgical nutritional counseling for 12 months.
plain_english: Health insurance covers bariatric surgery if your BMI is above 35 (or above 30 with related health issues like diabetes) AND you have tried supervised weight loss for 6 months without success. There is a 24-month waiting period before this benefit activates. The surgery itself and 12 months of post-surgical nutritional follow-up are covered.
consumer_tip: Bariatric surgery in India costs ₹2-5 lakh depending on the procedure and hospital. If you qualify (BMI > 35 or BMI > 30 with diabetes), start the 6-month supervised weight loss documentation immediately — this is a mandatory requirement. Keep all weight loss program records: dietitian consultations, gym memberships, medication, and periodic weight measurements. Without this 6-month documentation, your bariatric claim will be rejected.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Implant and Stent Coverage
content: All medically necessary implants shall be covered at the actual cost billed by the hospital, subject to the Sum Insured. Common implants covered: (a) Cardiac stents (bare metal and drug-eluting) at NPPA-capped prices; (b) Knee replacement implants (total and partial); (c) Hip replacement implants; (d) Cochlear implants; (e) Spinal implants (rods, screws, cages); (f) Intraocular lenses (IOL) for cataract surgery; (g) Pacemakers, ICDs, and CRT devices; (h) Bone plates and screws for fracture fixation. The insurer shall not specify the brand or type of implant — the treating surgeon's recommendation shall be followed.
plain_english: Your health insurance covers all medically necessary implants at actual cost. Cardiac stents have NPPA price caps (₹7,660 for bare metal, ₹29,600 for drug-eluting). Knee and hip replacement implants, cochlear implants, spinal hardware, pacemakers, and bone fixation devices are all covered. The insurer cannot dictate which brand or type of implant your doctor should use.
consumer_tip: Implant costs are a significant part of many surgical bills. A knee replacement implant alone costs ₹50,000 to ₹3 lakh depending on the type (cemented vs cementless, standard vs high-flex). Ensure your sum insured is adequate to cover the implant plus the overall surgical cost. If your insurer tries to restrict implant brand or limit payout to a 'standard' implant cost — this violates IRDAI guidelines. Your doctor's recommendation should be followed. Get the implant serial number and warranty card for future reference.

policy_name: Standard Motor Policy — Roadside Assistance Add-On
content: Subject to payment of additional premium, the Company shall provide the following roadside assistance services: (a) Towing: vehicle towed to nearest network garage or a garage of the Insured's choice within 50 km radius, at no cost; (b) Battery jump-start: on-site assistance for dead battery; (c) Flat tyre change: assistance with spare tyre fitting (tyre/tube cost not included); (d) Emergency fuel delivery: up to 5 liters of fuel delivered to stranded vehicle (fuel cost payable by Insured); (e) Lockout assistance: arrangement for locksmith; (f) Minor on-site repair: basic roadside repairs to enable vehicle movement; (g) Taxi benefit: reimbursement up to ₹1,500 for taxi to reach home/destination if vehicle cannot be moved.
plain_english: Roadside assistance add-on provides emergency help when your car breaks down: free towing within 50 km, battery jump-start, flat tyre change, emergency fuel delivery, and locksmith for lockout. If your car cannot be fixed on the spot, they arrange a taxi for you (up to ₹1,500 reimbursed). This is a convenience add-on — not a repair guarantee.
consumer_tip: Roadside assistance is one of the cheapest add-ons (₹200-500/year) and provides enormous peace of mind, especially for long highway drives. Save the roadside assistance helpline number in your phone. Note: roadside assistance covers getting your car moving again — not the actual repair cost (that is covered under OD). If you frequently drive on highways or in remote areas, this add-on is a must-have.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Policy Loan Facility
content: The Policyholder may avail a loan against the policy subject to: (a) the policy has been in force for a minimum of 3 consecutive years with all premiums paid; (b) the maximum loan amount shall not exceed 90% of the surrender value of the policy; (c) interest on the loan shall be charged at the rate determined by the Company (typically repo rate + 2-3%); (d) the loan and interest shall be deducted from any claim (death or maturity) payment; (e) if the loan plus accrued interest exceeds the surrender value, the policy shall be foreclosed and the policyholder informed. The loan facility is available only for traditional plans and not for ULIPs or term insurance.
plain_english: You can borrow money against your traditional life insurance policy (endowment, whole life, money back). The maximum loan is 90% of the surrender value. Interest is charged at repo rate + 2-3%. The loan does not need to be repaid in EMIs — the principal and interest are deducted from the maturity or death benefit. If you never repay and the loan grows larger than the surrender value, the policy is cancelled.
consumer_tip: A policy loan is often cheaper than a personal loan — interest rates are typically 8-10% compared to 12-18% for personal loans. It is also fast: no credit check, no income proof — the policy is the collateral. However, beware of compound interest accumulation: if you do not repay, the interest keeps adding up and can eat into your maturity benefit significantly. Always compare: taking a policy loan vs surrendering the policy and investing elsewhere.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Post-Hospitalization Coverage
content: The Company shall reimburse medical expenses incurred for treatment of the same ailment for which the Insured Person was hospitalized, for a period of 60 days (or 90 days as specified in the Schedule) from the date of discharge. Post-hospitalization expenses include: (a) Follow-up doctor consultations; (b) Diagnostic tests as prescribed by the treating doctor; (c) Medicines prescribed at discharge or during follow-up; (d) Physiotherapy sessions as prescribed. The expenses must be directly related to the condition for which hospitalization occurred. Receipts and prescriptions must be submitted as proof.
plain_english: After you are discharged from the hospital, your health insurance continues to cover related medical expenses for 60-90 days. This includes follow-up doctor visits, blood tests, medicines prescribed at discharge, and physiotherapy. The expenses must be for the SAME condition you were hospitalized for — you cannot claim unrelated expenses under post-hospitalization.
consumer_tip: Many policyholders forget to claim post-hospitalization expenses. After discharge, keep ALL receipts: pharmacy bills, doctor consultation fees, diagnostic reports, and physiotherapy bills. Submit these as part of your original claim within the claim submission deadline. For chronic conditions (cancer, cardiac), post-hospitalization expenses can be ₹20,000-50,000 — a significant amount worth claiming.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Pre-Hospitalization Coverage
content: The Company shall reimburse medical expenses incurred for treatment of the same ailment for which the Insured Person is subsequently hospitalized, for a period of 30 days (or 60 days as specified in the Schedule) prior to the date of admission. Pre-hospitalization expenses include: (a) Doctor consultations leading to hospitalization decision; (b) Diagnostic tests that led to diagnosis; (c) Medicines prescribed prior to admission; (d) Second opinion consultations. The expenses must be directly related to the condition for which hospitalization subsequently occurred.
plain_english: Medical expenses for up to 30-60 days before your hospitalization are also covered — including the doctor visits, blood tests, scans, and medicines that led to the diagnosis and hospital admission. If you visited a cardiologist who ordered an ECG and stress test, then referred you for angioplasty — those pre-hospital expenses are all covered.
consumer_tip: Start collecting receipts from the moment you first visit a doctor for the condition that eventually leads to hospitalization. Many people throw away initial consultation and diagnostic receipts — these are claimable under pre-hospitalization. If you had multiple doctor consultations across different specialties before reaching the final diagnosis, all related consultations are covered.

policy_name: Standard Travel Policy — Passport Loss Coverage
content: The Company shall reimburse expenses incurred by the Insured Person for obtaining a duplicate passport in the event of loss of passport during the trip: (a) Embassy/Consulate fees for emergency travel document or replacement passport; (b) Photographs and documentation costs; (c) Communication charges (phone, fax) related to passport replacement; (d) Additional hotel accommodation (up to 3 nights) while awaiting replacement passport; (e) Additional travel costs if the Insured misses their return flight due to passport replacement delay. Maximum coverage: ₹50,000 or as specified in the Schedule. Conditions: FIR or police report at the place of loss is mandatory.
plain_english: If you lose your passport abroad, travel insurance covers the cost of getting a replacement: embassy fees, photos, extra hotel stays while waiting for the new passport, and rebooking travel if you miss your flight. You must file a police report/FIR immediately at the place where the passport was lost or stolen.
consumer_tip: If your passport is lost or stolen abroad: (1) File a police report immediately, (2) Contact the nearest Indian Embassy/Consulate, (3) Keep all receipts for everything: hotel, food, communication, photos, fees. The maximum coverage is typically ₹50,000 — which should cover most expenses for passport replacement in most countries. Carry photocopies of your passport and visa separately from the originals.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — HIV/AIDS Coverage
content: In compliance with Section 3 of the HIV and AIDS (Prevention and Control) Act, 2017, the Company shall cover medical treatment for HIV/AIDS on the same basis as any other chronic disease. Coverage includes: (a) Hospitalization for HIV-related illnesses and opportunistic infections; (b) Antiretroviral therapy (ART) during hospitalization; (c) Diagnostic testing including CD4 count, viral load testing; (d) Treatment of opportunistic infections (tuberculosis, pneumonia, candidiasis) arising from HIV. The Company shall not: (i) deny coverage based on HIV status; (ii) charge additional premium based on HIV status; (iii) exclude HIV/AIDS from policy coverage.
plain_english: Health insurance must cover HIV/AIDS treatment — this is mandated by law. The insurer cannot deny you a policy, charge higher premium, or exclude HIV from coverage. Hospitalization for HIV-related conditions, antiretroviral therapy, and treatment of infections arising from HIV are all covered. Pre-existing disease waiting period applies (4 years) but cannot be extended beyond standard for HIV.
consumer_tip: If you are living with HIV and an insurer refuses to sell you health insurance or adds a specific HIV exclusion — this is illegal under the HIV/AIDS Act 2017 and IRDAI guidelines. File a complaint with IRDAI Bima Bharosa citing the Act. Many people with HIV do not buy health insurance thinking they are not eligible — you ARE eligible, and coverage is your legal right.

policy_name: Standard Life Policy — Waiver of Premium Rider
content: Waiver of Premium Rider: In the event of the Policyholder's Total Permanent Disability due to accident or diagnosis of any of the listed Critical Illnesses, all future premiums falling due under this policy shall be waived. The policy shall continue as if all premiums are being paid — all benefits including death benefit, maturity benefit, and riders shall remain in force. The waiver shall commence from the premium due date immediately following the date of disability/diagnosis and shall continue for the remaining premium payment term. This rider is available as an add-on at additional premium.
plain_english: If you become permanently disabled or are diagnosed with a critical illness, this rider waives all future premiums on your life policy. The policy continues with full benefits — your family's death benefit protection remains intact even though you are no longer paying premiums. This is crucial for scenarios where disability or serious illness eliminates your income.
consumer_tip: The waiver of premium rider is one of the most important riders for term insurance. If you are diagnosed with cancer or have a disabling accident, you likely cannot work and earn — how will you pay premiums? This rider ensures your family's ₹1 crore term cover continues even when you cannot pay. The cost is typically ₹500-1,500/year — absolutely worth it. It is especially important for policies with long premium payment terms (20-30 years).

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Claim Settlement Timelines (IRDAI Mandate)
content: The Company shall settle claims within the following timelines mandated by IRDAI: (a) Cashless pre-authorization: approval/rejection within 1 hour for planned procedures, 30 minutes for emergencies; (b) Cashless final settlement: within 3 hours of discharge; (c) Reimbursement claims: within 30 days of receiving complete documents; (d) Additional document request: within 7 days of claim receipt; (e) Rejection communication: within 30 days with specific reasons citing policy clause. Interest at 2% above bank rate shall be payable on delayed settlement beyond these timelines. Any breach shall be reported to the IRDAI Policyholder Protection Division.
plain_english: IRDAI has set strict timelines for claim settlement. Cashless must be approved within 1 hour (30 minutes for emergencies). Reimbursement must be settled within 30 days of complete documentation. If the insurer takes longer, they must pay you interest. If they reject your claim, they must give specific reasons citing the exact policy clause — generic rejection is not permitted.
consumer_tip: Know your timelines and assert them: if cashless pre-auth takes more than 1 hour, escalate to the insurer's senior management citing IRDAI mandate. If reimbursement takes more than 30 days, demand interest. If rejection does not cite a specific policy clause, challenge it as non-compliant with IRDAI guidelines. Keep a timeline record of all communications with your insurer — dates of submission, responses, and delays. This documentation is invaluable if you escalate to Ombudsman or consumer forum.

policy_name: Standard Health Policy — Portability Clause
content: The Insured Person has the right to port this policy to any other insurer under the IRDAI Health Insurance Portability Guidelines. On porting: (a) All waiting period credit earned with the current insurer shall be fully transferred to the new insurer; (b) The new insurer must accept portability for the same or lower sum insured without fresh medical underwriting; (c) Cumulative bonus / NCB earned shall be credited by the new insurer; (d) The portability window is 45 days before to 30 days after the renewal date; (e) The current insurer must issue a portability certificate within 7 days of request. Portability does not apply to: products with fundamentally different structures (e.g., indemnity to benefit-based).
plain_english: You can switch your health insurance from one company to another (portability) without losing your waiting period credit. If you have served 3 years of PED waiting with your current insurer, the new insurer must credit those 3 years. Your NCB/cumulative bonus also transfers. The switch must happen within a specific window around your renewal date. Your current insurer must give you a portability certificate within 7 days.
consumer_tip: Portability is your power tool against bad service. If your insurer has poor claim settlement, delayed cashless, or unhelpful customer service — port to a better insurer at renewal. You do not lose any waiting period credit or NCB. Start the portability process 45 days before renewal: (1) Apply to the new insurer, (2) Request portability certificate from current insurer, (3) New insurer must respond within 15 days. Do not cancel your existing policy until the new policy is confirmed.
